content:
# Leviticus Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58v–59r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_003_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_003_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** If someone's offering is a fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering — and they bring it from the herd, whether male or female, they shall bring an animal without defect before the LORD. **2** They shall lay their hand on the head of their offering and slaughter it at the entrance to the tent of meeting. Then Aaron's sons the priests shall splash the blood against all sides of the altar. **3** From the fellowship sacrifice they shall present as a fire-offering to the LORD: the fat that covers the inner organs, all the fat that clings to them, **4** both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which they shall remove with the kidneys. **5** Aaron's sons shall burn this on the altar on top of the burnt offering that lies on the wood of the fire — it is a fire-offering, a fragrant aroma to the LORD.

**6** If their fellowship offering to the LORD is taken from the flock, whether male or female, it must be brought without defect. **7** If they bring a lamb as their offering, they shall present it before the LORD, **8** lay their hand on the head of their offering, and slaughter it in front of the tent of meeting. Aaron's sons shall splash its blood against all sides of the altar. **9** From the fellowship sacrifice they shall present the following as a fire-offering to the LORD: the fat — the entire fat tail, which they shall remove close to the backbone — together with the fat that covers the inner organs, all the fat that clings to them, **10** both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which they shall remove with the kidneys. **11** The priest shall burn it on the altar — it is the food of the fire-offering to the LORD.

**12** If their offering is a goat, they shall bring it before the LORD, **13** lay their hand on its head, and slaughter it in front of the tent of meeting. Aaron's sons shall splash its blood against all sides of the altar. **14** From it they shall present their offering as a fire-offering to the LORD: the fat that covers the inner organs, all the fat that clings to them, **15** both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which they shall remove with the kidneys. **16** The priest shall burn these on the altar — food of the fire-offering, a fragrant aroma. All the fat belongs to the LORD.

**17** This is a perpetual statute throughout your generations, wherever you live: you must not eat any fat or any blood.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Zevach shelamim — the meal you share with God.** The phrase that names this offering is *zevach shelamim*, and no single English word catches it. *Zevach* means a slaughter-offering — an animal killed for eating, not merely burned. *Shelamim* is the plural of *shalom*, and *shalom* is not just "peace" in the negative sense of an absence of conflict. It is wholeness, completeness, well-being, the flourishing of every relationship properly ordered. The *shelamim* is the offering of the person whose world is in right relationship with God — or who is bringing it precisely to restore that relationship. PNEUMA renders it "fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering" on first appearance (v. 1) to carry both registers: the relational warmth of shared table (fellowship) and the deeper Hebrew freight of *shalom*. After that, "fellowship offering" alone. The reason this offering is singular in the whole sacrificial system is that it is the only one where the worshiper actually eats. God gets the fat; the priest gets a portion; and the family sits down together for a covenant meal. It is a dinner party hosted by the LORD.

**Chelev — the fat belongs entirely to God.** Verse 16 closes with the declarative sentence that governs the entire chapter: *kol-chelev laYHWH* — "all the fat belongs to the LORD." The *chelev* here is not the marbled fat running through the muscle (which people ate freely); it is the hard suet, the organ fat — the covering of the stomach, the kidneys, the loins. In the ancient world, fat was the richest, most calorie-dense, most prized part of the animal. To give it entirely to God was to give the best. The burnt altar-fat rising in smoke is, in symbolic terms, God eating at the feast. The worshiper sits at the same table, but the choicest cut goes to the host. PNEUMA preserves "fat" throughout rather than the more clinical "suet" or "adipose tissue," because the ancient instinct — best food to God — is what drives the legislation.

**The fat tail (v. 9) — a detail that places you in the ancient world.** The sheep offering (vv. 6–11) has one striking detail absent from the herd and goat offerings: *ha-alyah* — the fat tail. This is the broad, thick, fatty tail of the Awassi or Dumba sheep, a breed still common in the ancient Near East, whose tail can weigh four to eight kilograms of pure fat. A modern Western reader may miss this entirely; an ancient Israelite reader would immediately picture a specific, well-known animal. This fat tail — the sheep's most prized cut, a delicacy in the ancient Near East to this day — belongs to God. PNEUMA renders it "the entire fat tail" to preserve both its specificity and its extravagance as an offering.

**"Food of the fire-offering" (v. 11) — does God eat?** The phrase *lechem isheh laYHWH* — "food of the fire-offering to the LORD" — appears twice in this chapter (vv. 11, 16). It is startling: the offering is described as *lechem*, bread, food, that which is eaten. The sacrificial system has an almost physical dimension to it — the burning fat is the meal that rises to God. Monotheistic Israel is clearly uncomfortable with crude anthropomorphism, and later rabbinic interpretation heavily spiritualized this language. But the text lets it stand. PNEUMA renders it straightforwardly — "the food of the fire-offering to the LORD" — because the chapter is making a point about a shared table, and softening the language obscures the very meaning that makes the *shelamim* distinctive.

**Chuqqat olam — perpetual statute (v. 17).** The chapter closes with the most severe legislative language in the Torah: *chuqqat olam ledoroteikhem* — a statute for eternity across all your generations. The word *chuqqah* is used for the most binding, most unconditional class of law — not case law, not instruction, but decree. *Olam* is the word for deep time, eternity, the horizon that recedes forever. The fat-and-blood prohibition at v. 17 is not presented as a cultural rule or a health regulation; it is framed as a cosmic constitutional clause. PNEUMA renders it "a perpetual statute throughout your generations, wherever you live" — the final phrase (*bechol moshevoteikhem*, "in all your dwelling-places") expanding the prohibition beyond the sanctuary into every home, every meal, every table in Israel. The covenant that God hosts at the altar follows Israel all the way home.

**Why blood and fat together?** Verse 17 prohibits two things: fat and blood. The fat is God's portion at the feast. The blood, as Leviticus will explain more fully (17:11), is the life itself — *ki haneshamah badam* — "the life is in the blood." To eat blood is not merely to break a dietary rule; it is to consume life, which belongs to God alone. Fat and blood are paired here because they are the two elements that cross the boundary between creature and Creator. The fat is the richest food; the blood is life. Both belong to the One who gave the animal its richness and its breath. Israel may feast at God's table, but two things are always set aside.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations.** Codex Vaticanus pages 91–92 preserve Leviticus 3 in clean uncial script. The LXX translates *zevach shelamim* as *θυσία σωτηρίου* (*thusia soteriou* — "sacrifice of salvation/deliverance"), a striking Greek interpretive move. Where Hebrew *shalom* points toward wholeness and well-being, Greek *soteria* pulls toward rescue and preservation from danger — a slightly more dramatic register. The LXX rendering may reflect a Hellenistic Jewish reading of the *shelamim* not merely as a well-being celebration but as a thank-offering for deliverance. This distinction matters for understanding how the *shelamim* tradition flowed into early Christian eucharistic language — the "peace offering" of the Lord's Table carries both the Hebrew wholeness and the Greek deliverance. At v. 16 the Vaticanus reads *πᾶν τὸ στέαρ τῷ κυρίῳ* (*pan to stear to kyrio* — "all the fat to the LORD"), precisely matching the Hebrew *kol-chelev laYHWH* — a clean, declarative confirmation that leaves nothing ambiguous. The fat tail (*alyah*) in v. 9 is rendered *ὀσφύν* (*osphyn* — loin/haunch) in the LXX, a partial translation that loses the specific breed-particularity of the Hebrew; the Vaticanus scribe makes no correction here. At v. 17 the LXX renders *chuqqat olam* as *νόμιμον αἰώνιον* (*nomimon aionion* — "everlasting ordinance"), using *aionion* (from *aion*, age/eternity) — the same word that will appear in the New Testament for "eternal life." The fat-and-blood prohibition is thus framed in both traditions in the language of eternity.

*— PNEUMA*
